value.Title: Turkish Firms Investigate US Investment Opportunities

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ0-XF63-S3CF-H0SS-00000-00Turkish Firms Investigate US Investment Opportunities2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:23ZLexisNexisTurkish Firms Investigate US Investment Opportunities

Link to Image

Turkish entrepreneurs held meetings with delegates from various US states during this week’s SelectUSA Investment Summit, aiming to examine potential investment prospects across a wide range of industries, including renewable energy, robotics, health care technology, and dental services.



The summit took place from May 3 to May 6 in Maryland, near Washington, DC, and gathered thousands of attendees such as investors, economic development officials, and sector specialists representing more than 100 countries.

Over 30 companies from Türkiye participated in the gathering, reflecting growing interest in expanding business ties with the United States.

Tarik Sarvan, chairman of the prominent Turkish solar energy company CW Enerji, noted that the company currently exports approximately $5–$6 million worth of goods each month from Türkiye to the US and is evaluating investment possibilities on a state-by-state basis. He stated, “We are trying to create a gateway where we can sell our products,” emphasizing that manufacturing activities would continue in Türkiye while any US-based operations would be limited to assembly and sales.

Bakit Baydaliev, chief executive of the technology company DOF Robotics, explained that the United States already represents 40% of the company’s market share, a figure that continues to rise steadily. He added that the firm is planning to expand its immersive entertainment operations across several US states, including Montana, Wyoming, and Colorado.MENAFN09052026000045017167ID1111089585

value.Title: Krambu: 100 megawatts possible

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGS-D1D3-S76K-80M5-00000-00Krambu: 100 megawatts possible2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisMISSOULIAN.COMKrambu: 100 megawatts possibleGRIFFEN SMITH griffen.smith@missoulian.com

The developers of a proposed artificial intelligence data center in Bonner told Missoula County the facility could use up 100 megawatts after a "full build out" of the site, according to public records filed this week.

Data center company Krambu announced in March that it seeks to use vacant space within the Bonner Mill Industrial Park for an "AI factory", which still needs to undergo a special zoning review before the plans can move forward.

While Krambu representatives initially told Bonner residents the development would start at 3 megawatts, the company also acknowledged it could grow the center over several years.

In a supplemental information packet to the county, Krambu-contracted company Dobler Engineering wrote that the space currently has capacity for 29 megawatts.

"It's important to note that full build out of the site may take two to three years, and that depends on the ultimate amount of power available to the site (possibly up to 100 MW) and the market demand," the company wrote in the document on April 27.

100 megawatts could power somewhere between 20,000 and 100,000 homes, depending on the power source, and would generally be classified as an average-sized data center, according to IBM.

The Missoulian first reported on Krambu's initial application through a public records request, which showed the company does not plan to regularly staff the site once construction is completed.

Krambu CEO Steve Wood did not return a call from the Missoulian on Friday seeking comment.

Missoula County has requested additional information from Krambu before a public review process can begin on the proposal. On Wednesday, county planner Jennie Dixon told the company that more work needs to be done on the possible noise, visual, water, and heat criteria.

"With materials submitted thus far, I do not have enough information to make a favorable recommendation to the Land Use Board on the special exception," Dixon wrote to Krambu on April 29. "My goal is to assist you in preparing an application that will address all potential concerns that might be raised in review of the special exception."

The county said on its website that the earliest a special exception review hearing could be held before the Missoula County Consolidated Land Use Board would be on July 1.

The land use board would address whether the proposal would create a negative impact on nearby residents. The proposal does not need approval from the Board of Missoula County Commissioners to proceed.

County regulations also require Krambu secure a new source of renewable energy to supply the project and recycle electronic waste. Those regulations came in response to a cryptocurrency mining operation at the site several years ago.

In its most recent filings with the county, Dobler Engineering said Krambu's water use would involve a closed loop system that limits the amount of water it uses to about 2,000 gallons per year.

"The evaporative coolers are constructed like a car radiator and drip water over the cooling fluid lines. The water is collected in a reservoir at the bottom and recirculated," the document said.

The system would require evaporative cooling towers to be built on the exterior of the Bonner mill building. Each tower would cool the equivalent of 4 to 5 megawatts, according to the application.

Currently, Dobler Engineering said 10 towers would be required for the current energy capacity of the facility. The cooling structures would not be taller than the existing building.

The data center would be powered 24 hours a day and have a safeguard system to keep the operation active in the event of maintenance issues, but the company does not plan to have a backup power generator on site, according to the application.

Krambu proposes a row of street trees to minimize the impact of new construction on nearby residences. Wood also told Bonner residents he hopes to add additional uses to the site, like a hydroponics garden.

A petition on change.org urging the county to reject the permits for the data center had secured roughly 2,400 signatures as of Friday afternoon.

Data centers have drawn more public attention in the last two years as applications for them increase across the country, including in talking points for candidates running for public office in Montana.

More information can also be found at MissoulaCountyVoice.com.

Griff en Smith is the local government reporter for the Missoulian.

The land use board would address whether the proposal would create a negative impact on nearby residents. The proposal does not need approval from the Board of Missoula County Commissioners to proceed.

County regulations also require Krambu secure a new source of renewable energy to supply the project and recycle electronic waste. Those regulations came in response to a cryptocurrency mining operation at the site several years ago.

In its most recent filings with the county, Dobler Engineering said Krambu's water use would involve a closed loop system that limits the amount of water it uses to about 2,000 gallons per year.

"The evaporative coolers are constructed like a car radiator and drip water over the cooling fluid lines. The water is collected in a reservoir at the bottom and recirculated," the document said.

The system would require evaporative cooling towers to be built on the exterior of the Bonner mill building. Each tower would cool the equivalent of 4 to 5 megawatts, according to the application.

Currently, Dobler Engineering said 10 towers would be required for the current energy capacity of the facility. The cooling structures would not be taller than the existing building.

The data center would be powered 24 hours a day and have a safeguard system to keep the operation active in the event of maintenance issues, but the company does not plan to have a backup power generator on site, according to the application.

Krambu proposes a row of street trees to minimize the impact of new construction on nearby residences. Wood also told Bonner residents he hopes to add additional uses to the site, like a hydroponics garden.

A petition on change.org urging the county to reject the permits for the data center had secured roughly 2,400 signatures as of Friday afternoon.

Data centers have drawn more public attention in the last two years as applications for them increase across the country, including in talking points for candidates running for public office in Montana.

More information can also be found at MissoulaCountyVoice.com.

value.Title: Proposed Bonner data center company seeks 100 megawatt facility

urn:contentItem:6JGJ-DXR3-S0NC-D4S1-00000-00Proposed Bonner data center company seeks 100 megawatt facility2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisLOCALProposed Bonner data center company seeks 100 megawatt facilityGRIFFEN SMITH griffen.smith@missoulian.com

The developers of a proposed artificial intelligence data center in Bonner told Missoula County the facility could use up 100 megawatts after a "full build out" of the site, according to public records filed this week.

Data center company Krambu announced in March that it seeks to use vacant space within the Bonner Mill Industrial Park for an "AI factory", which still needs to undergo a special zoning review before the plans can move forward.

While Krambu representatives initially told Bonner residents the development would start at 3 megawatts, the company also acknowledged it could grow the center over several years.

In a supplemental information packet to the county, Krambu-contracted company Dobler Engineering wrote that the space currently has capacity for 29 megawatts.

"It's important to note that full build out of the site may take two to three years, and that depends on the ultimate amount of power available to the site (possibly up to 100 MW) and the market demand," the company wrote in the document on April 27.

100 megawatts could power somewhere between 20,000 and 100,000 homes, depending on the power source, and would generally be classified as an average-sized data center, according to IBM.

The Missoulian first reported on Krambu's initial application through a public records request, which showed the company does not plan to regularly staff the site once construction is completed.

Krambu CEO Steve Wood did not return a call from the Missoulian on Friday seeking comment.

Missoula County has requested additional information from Krambu before a public review process can begin on the proposal. On Wednesday, county planner Jennie Dixon told the company that more work needs to be done on the possible noise, visual, water, and heat criteria.

"With materials submitted thus far, I do not have enough information to make a favorable recommendation to the Land Use Board on the special exception," Dixon wrote to Krambu on April 29. "My goal is to assist you in preparing an application that will address all potential concerns that might be raised in review of the special exception."

The county said on its website that the earliest a special exception review hearing could be held before the Missoula County Consolidated Land Use Board would be on July 1.

The land use board would address whether the proposal would create a negative impact on nearby residents. The proposal does not need approval from the Board of Missoula County Commissioners to proceed.

County regulations also require Krambu secure a new source of renewable energy to supply the project and recycle electronic waste. Those regulations came in response to a cryptocurrency mining operation at the site several years ago.

In its most recent filings with the county, Dobler Engineering said Krambu's water use would involve a closed loop system that limits the amount of water it uses to about 2,000 gallons per year.

"The evaporative coolers are constructed like a car radiator and drip water over the cooling fluid lines. The water is collected in a reservoir at the bottom and recirculated," the document said.

The system would require evaporative cooling towers to be built on the exterior of the Bonner mill building. Each tower would cool the equivalent of 4 to 5 megawatts, according to the application.

Currently, Dobler Engineering said 10 towers would be required for the current energy capacity of the facility. The cooling structures would not be taller than the existing building.

The data center would be powered 24 hours a day and have a safeguard system to keep the operation active in the event of maintenance issues, but the company does not plan to have a backup power generator on site, according to the application.

Krambu proposes a row of street trees to minimize the impact of new construction on nearby residences. Wood also told Bonner residents he hopes to add additional uses to the site, like a hydroponics garden.

A petition on change.org urging the county to reject the permits for the data center had secured roughly 2,400 signatures as of Friday afternoon.

Data centers have drawn more public attention in the last two years as applications for them increase across the country, including in talking points for candidates running for public office in Montana.

More information can also be found at MissoulaCountyVoice.com.

value.Title: Bridger Photonics Expands into RNG with New Solution Delivering Full-Site Methane Visibility Across Landfill, Wastewater, and Agriculture

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-S7S3-S7JJ-D486-00000-00Bridger Photonics Expands into RNG with New Solution Delivering Full-Site Methane Visibility Across Landfill, Wastewater, and Agriculture2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBridger Photonics Expands into RNG with New Solution Delivering Full-Site Methane Visibility Across Landfill, Wastewater, and Agriculture

Key Highlights:

* The solution provides quantified, whole-site methane measurement and individual emission location data for operators.

* Bridger for RNG helps operators protect revenue, reduce regulatory exposure, and make smarter financial decisions.

* The solution uses Gas Mapping LiDAR technology for complete, quantified emissions data that is auditable and ready for review.

Original Press Release:

Bozeman, Montana, May 12 -- Bridger Photonics Incissued the following news release:

Bridger Photonics ("Bridger"), the global leader in methane emissions detection and intelligence, today announced the launch of Bridger for RNG-entering the renewable natural gas market with complete coverage across landfill, wastewater, and agriculture, powered by a single, proven platform.

Bridger's solution, tuned for RNG, offers monitoring with complete, defensible insight that helps operators protect revenue, reduce regulatory exposure, and make smarter financial decisions.

Landfill operators make critical decisions every day across gas capture, compliance, capital allocation, and RNG optimization. Yet many continue to rely on partial measurements and modeled estimates that leave significant uncertainty about what their sites are truly emitting, and where the individual sources are. Bridger enables operators to solve this problem, providing quantified, whole-site methane measurement, as well as individual emission location data that enables operators to move from uncertainty to financial and operational control.

"Unmeasured methane represents both lost RNG value and increased regulatory risk," said Ben Little, CEO of Bridger Photonics. "Bridger for RNG gives landfill, wastewater, and agricultural operators a quantified understanding of emissions across their entire site, so they can protect project economics, run their operations more efficiently, and confidently stand behind their data."

Bridger for RNG delivers quantified methane emissions across the full asset footprint, regardless of size, terrain, or complexity. Using Bridger's proven Gas Mapping LiDAR technology that has been extensively adopted globally across the natural gas value chain, the company's RNG solutions provide complete, quantified emissions data that is auditable and ready for regulatory and third-party review, including for carbon intensity reporting.

As part of Bridger's expanding emissions intelligence offerings, the latest solutions for RNG reflect a continued investment in turning high-quality measurement into real-world impact. The solution enables operators to move beyond incomplete data and reactive workflows toward proactive, measurement-driven methane management that protects value and reduces risk.

About Bridger Photonics

Bridger Photonics delivers trusted emissions data and intelligence to support confident decision-making at scale. Proven across the oil and natural gas value chain, Bridger's solutions provide year-round coverage across assets, locations, and seasons, helping operators improve efficiency, reduce emissions, and recover value. Bridger is trusted by leading energy companies, including ExxonMobil, Chevron, and Phillips 66.

[Category: Agriculture, Renewable Energy, General Public Services, New Offerings, Technology, ESG and Sustainability]

Source: Bridger Photonics Inc

value.Title: Quantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near Broadview

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JKB-BV63-S7DD-P026-00000-00Quantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near Broadview2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisNEWSQuantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near BroadviewALEX MITCHELL amitchell@billingsgazette.com

Quantica Infrastructure reaffirmed Thursday a "pay our own way" approach to powering its planned 5,100-acre data center campus north of Billings.

In a press release, Quantica pledged to cover the costs of electricity infrastructure and any new generation needed to serve its project amid concerns around how NorthWestern Energy might profit off data centers by pass the costs to Montana ratepayers.

"We're paying our own way for power, without increasing Montanans' electricity bills for our project," Quantica Infrastructure CEO John Chesser said in a statement. "We'll pay the cost of electricity infrastructure and any new power generation needed to serve our project. We'll continue working with utilities and regulators to ensure the project is responsibly reviewed, permitted and aligned with the community's priorities."

The data center, contingent on attaining a customer, plans to rely on a mix of their own behind-the-meter wind and solar assets obtained years ago in addition to power from NorthWestern Energy.

Chesser said they are pursuing an all-of-the-above power portfolio, including traditional energy resources, in addition to battery energy storage. He also noted the "evaluation of emerging energy technologies" to power the data center.

Montana Environmental Information Center director Anne Hedges considered the press release to be a "change in their tune" from what Quantica has relayed to the public in its initial messaging.

"They used to say it would all be renewable energy and storage the lowest cost energy sources today," Hedges said. "Now they are saying traditional energy which means coal and gas. Those resources impose a huge impact on the state of Montana and across the globe, including climate change, air pollution and water pollution."

She added that the emerging technologies reference mentioned in the press release are a "modern day euphemism for nuclear power." Chesser was previously involved in the reported first nuclear-powered data center in the U.S.

In a response about the referenced emerging energy technologies, Quantica stated: "We are evaluating tools that can make the project more reliable and lower ‑carbon over time, including advanced long‑duration storage, grid‑optimization technologies, and other lower‑carbon firm resources. Finally, we appreciate the opportunity to clarify, as many Montanans have concerns and expect these discussions to be honest and straightforward, not clouded with speculation and exaggeration, as some opponents of responsible development are prone to."

NorthWestern has agreed to provide up to 1,000 megawatts to the data center, though initial deployment is planned to be 500 megawatts. Where exactly that power will come from hasn't been shared publicly. At the start of this year, NorthWestern acquired a 375 megawatt share of Colstrip it will sell power from on the market.

The press release echoes a sentiment shared by Quantica's leaders months ago about a desire for the company to pay its fair share.

However, the CEO's statement comes amid a hearing held by the Public Service Commission over a planned merger of NorthWestern and Black Hills Energy. NorthWestern CEO Brian Bird has publicly stated that the merger will give the monopoly utility "better opportunities" around data centers.

Opponents have used that focus on data centers to assess that the merger's approval would lead to higher costs by facilitating and encouraging further data center development down the line, potentially leading to infrastructure costs shouldered by ratepayers.

The statement also arrives during grassroots animus against data centers. A Gallup poll released Wednesday found 71% of Americans oppose local construction of data centers.

Quantica's press release posted online only mentions the word "data center" once in a description of Big Sky Digital Infrastructure, which plans to build the project.

Chesser has previously stated that they expect to begin leveling ground and placing roads this year. In 2027, the company expects to lay the foundations of some buildings. And by 2028, the company expects to begin drawing power for its data center.

Quantica still hasn't obtained a customer, but the company said it continues to see "strong interest in Montana."

value.Title: Quantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near Broadview

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JKB-27S3-S8PY-S1GC-00000-00Quantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near Broadview2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisNEWSQuantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near BroadviewALEX MITCHELL amitchell@billingsgazette.com

Quantica Infrastructure reaffirmed Thursday a "pay our own way" approach to powering its planned 5,100-acre data center campus north of Billings.

In a press release, Quantica pledged to cover the costs of electricity infrastructure and any new generation needed to serve its project amid concerns around how NorthWestern Energy might profit off data centers by pass the costs to Montana ratepayers.

"We're paying our own way for power, without increasing Montanans' electricity bills for our project," Quantica Infrastructure CEO John Chesser said in a statement. "We'll pay the cost of electricity infrastructure and any new power generation needed to serve our project. We'll continue working with utilities and regulators to ensure the project is responsibly reviewed, permitted and aligned with the community's priorities."

The data center, contingent on attaining a customer, plans to rely on a mix of their own behind-the-meter wind and solar assets obtained years ago in addition to power from NorthWestern Energy.

Chesser said they are pursuing an all-of-the-above power portfolio, including traditional energy resources, in addition to battery energy storage. He also noted the "evaluation of emerging energy technologies" to power the data center.

Montana Environmental Information Center director Anne Hedges considered the press release to be a "change in their tune" from what Quantica has relayed to the public in its initial messaging.

"They used to say it would all be renewable energy and storage the lowest cost energy sources today," Hedges said. "Now they are saying traditional energy which means coal and gas. Those resources impose a huge impact on the state of Montana and across the globe, including climate change, air pollution and water pollution."

She added that the emerging technologies reference mentioned in the press release are a "modern day euphemism for nuclear power." Chesser was previously involved in the reported first nuclear-powered data center in the U.S.

In a response about the referenced emerging energy technologies, Quantica stated: "We are evaluating tools that can make the project more reliable and lower ‑carbon over time, including advanced long‑duration storage, grid‑optimization technologies, and other lower‑carbon firm resources. Finally, we appreciate the opportunity to clarify, as many Montanans have concerns and expect these discussions to be honest and straightforward, not clouded with speculation and exaggeration, as some opponents of responsible development are prone to."

NorthWestern has agreed to provide up to 1,000 megawatts to the data center, though initial deployment is planned to be 500 megawatts. Where exactly that power will come from hasn't been shared publicly. At the start of this year, NorthWestern acquired a 375 megawatt share of Colstrip it will sell power from on the market.

The press release echoes a sentiment shared by Quantica's leaders months ago about a desire for the company to pay its fair share.

However, the CEO's statement comes amid a hearing held by the Public Service Commission over a planned merger of NorthWestern and Black Hills Energy. NorthWestern CEO Brian Bird has publicly stated that the merger will give the monopoly utility "better opportunities" around data centers.

Opponents have used that focus on data centers to assess that the merger's approval would lead to higher costs by facilitating and encouraging further data center development down the line, potentially leading to infrastructure costs shouldered by ratepayers.

The statement also arrives during grassroots animus against data centers. A Gallup poll released Wednesday found 71% of Americans oppose local construction of data centers.

Quantica's press release posted online only mentions the word "data center" once in a description of Big Sky Digital Infrastructure, which plans to build the project.

Chesser has previously stated that they expect to begin leveling ground and placing roads this year. In 2027, the company expects to lay the foundations of some buildings. And by 2028, the company expects to begin drawing power for its data center.

Quantica still hasn't obtained a customer, but the company said it continues to see "strong interest in Montana."

"We'll pay the cost of electricity infrastructure and any new power generation needed to serve our project. We'll continue working with utilities and regulators to ensure the project is responsibly reviewed, permitted and aligned with the community's priorities."

The data center, contingent on attaining a customer, plans to rely on a mix of their own behind-the-meter wind and solar assets obtained years ago in addition to power from NorthWestern Energy.

Chesser said they are pursuing an all-of-the-above power portfolio, including traditional energy resources, in addition to battery energy storage. He also noted the "evaluation of emerging energy technologies" to power the data center.

Montana Environmental Information Center director Anne Hedges considered the press release to be a "change in their tune" from what Quantica has relayed to the public in its initial messaging.

"They used to say it would all be renewable energy and storage the lowest cost energy sources today," Hedges said. "Now they are saying traditional energy which means coal and gas. Those resources impose a huge impact on the state of Montana and across the globe, including climate change, air pollution and water pollution."

She added that the emerging technologies reference mentioned in the press release are a "modern day euphemism for nuclear power." Chesser was previously involved in the reported first nuclear-powered data center in the U.S.

In a response about the referenced emerging energy technologies, Quantica stated: "We are evaluating tools that can make the project more reliable and lower‑carbon over time, including advanced long‑duration storage, grid‑optimization technologies, and other lower‑carbon firm resources. Finally, we appreciate the opportunity to clarify, as many Montanans have concerns and expect these discussions to be honest and straightforward, not clouded with speculation and exaggeration, as some opponents of responsible development are prone to."

NorthWestern has agreed to provide up to 1,000 megawatts to the data center, though initial deployment is planned to be 500 megawatts. Where exactly that power will come from hasn't been shared publicly. At the start of this year, NorthWestern acquired a 375 megawatt share of Colstrip it will sell power from on the market.

The press release echoes a sentiment shared by Quantica's leaders months ago about a desire for the company to pay its fair share.

However, the CEO's statement comes amid a hearing held by the Public Service Commission over a planned merger of NorthWestern and Black Hills Energy. NorthWestern CEO Brian Bird has publicly stated that the merger will give the monopoly utility "better opportunities" around data centers.

Opponents have used that focus on data centers to assess that the merger's approval would lead to higher costs by facilitating and encouraging further data center development down the line, potentially leading to infrastructure costs shouldered by ratepayers.

The statement also arrives during grassroots animus against data centers. A Gallup poll released Wednesday found 71% of Americans oppose local construction of data centers.

Quantica's press release posted online only mentions the word "data center" once in a description of Big Sky Digital Infrastructure, which plans to build the project.

Chesser has previously stated that they expect to begin leveling ground and placing roads this year. In 2027, the company expects to lay the foundations of some buildings. And by 2028, the company expects to begin drawing power for its data center.

Quantica still hasn't obtained a customer, but the company said it continues to see "strong interest in Montana."

value.Title: Two Decades of Solar Expertise Powering Montana's Future

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHJ-0NG3-S438-J0P2-00000-00Two Decades of Solar Expertise Powering Montana's Future2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisCONTENTTwo Decades of Solar Expertise Powering Montana's FutureAdvertiser Content from FREEDOM SOLUTIONS

Montana's energy landscape demands more than cookie-cutter solutions. Between harsh winters, remote locations and rising utility costs, property owners need solar energy partners who understand the terrain literally and figuratively.

FREEDOM SOLUTIONS brings over 20 years of experience to every solar panel installation across Montana. That depth of knowledge matters when temperatures plunge below zero or summer storms test electrical systems. The Missoula-based company has spent two decades refining its approach to residential solar and commercial solar projects throughout the state.

Experience That Translates to Results

Twenty years in the solar energy Montana market means FREEDOM SOLUTIONS has seen technology evolve, costs drop and efficiency soar. The team has installed systems through economic shifts, policy changes and technological advances. That institutional knowledge helps property owners avoid costly mistakes and maximize returns on their investments.

The company handles the full spectrum of solar-related services. Solar panel installation forms the foundation, but FREEDOM SOLUTIONS also provides battery backup systems, whole home generators, EV charging stations and roof restoration. As licensed electrical contractors, they ensure every component integrates seamlessly with existing electrical infrastructure.

Comprehensive Solutions Under One Roof

Property owners often juggle multiple contractors when transitioning to renewable energy. One company handles the solar panels, another manages electrical work, and a third deals with structural concerns. FREEDOM SOLUTIONS consolidates those services.

The electrical contractor expertise proves particularly valuable. Solar installations require precise electrical work to ensure safety and optimal performance. Having that capability in-house eliminates coordination headaches and potential finger-pointing between contractors when issues arise.

Battery storage solutions have become increasingly important as Montana property owners seek true energy independence. FREEDOM SOLUTIONS designs systems that keep lights on during grid outages while storing excess solar production for nighttime use. These systems provide peace of mind during severe weather events that can leave conventional grid-tied homes in the dark.

Serving Diverse Property Needs

Residential solar projects differ significantly from commercial solar installations. Homeowners typically prioritize aesthetics, roof protection and long-term savings. Commercial properties focus on ROI, operational continuity and sustainability credentials.

FREEDOM SOLUTIONS adapts its approach based on property type and owner priorities. A ranch house outside Missoula requires different planning than a downtown commercial building. The company's experience across property types ensures appropriate solutions rather than one-size-fits-all recommendations.

EV charging stations represent another growing service area. As more Montanans drive electric vehicles, home charging infrastructure becomes essential. FREEDOM SOLUTIONS integrates EV charging with solar systems, allowing property owners to power their vehicles with clean energy generated on-site.

Montana-Specific Expertise

Solar energy Montana installations face unique challenges. Snow loads, extreme temperature swings and variable sunlight hours require careful system design. FREEDOM SOLUTIONS understands these regional factors from years of local experience.

Roof restoration services complement solar installations. Before mounting panels, roofs need structural integrity to support equipment for 25-plus years. The company assesses roof conditions and performs necessary repairs, ensuring solar arrays have solid foundations.

The financing landscape has evolved dramatically over 20 years. FREEDOM SOLUTIONS helps property owners navigate options that make solar accessible without prohibitive upfront costs. Understanding available incentives, tax credits and financing structures requires staying current with constantly changing policies.

Building Long-Term Relationships

Two decades in business means FREEDOM SOLUTIONS has maintained systems installed years ago. That long-term perspective influences initial installation quality. The company knows its work will face Montana's elements for decades, motivating attention to detail that fly-by-night installers might skip.

The team's longevity also provides continuity. Property owners can return to the same company for system expansions, maintenance or upgrades. That ongoing relationship ensures consistent service quality and institutional knowledge about specific installations.

Taking the Next Step

Property owners considering solar panel installation, battery backup systems or related services can connect with FREEDOM SOLUTIONS through multiple channels. Visit gofreedomenergy.com for detailed information about services and project examples. Call 640-252-5056 to discuss specific property needs and explore options.

The company maintains active social media presence on Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn and YouTube, offering insights into recent projects and energy independence strategies.

Twenty years of solar energy Montana experience means FREEDOM SOLUTIONS has helped hundreds of property owners reduce utility costs while increasing energy independence. That track record speaks louder than marketing promises.

value.Title: Letter to the Editor: Ordinance 2026-01 will create more red tape

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHS-00N3-RS1N-01N8-00000-00Letter to the Editor: Ordinance 2026-01 will create more red tape2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisCOMMENTARYLetter to the Editor: Ordinance 2026-01 will create more red tapeStaff Writer

The Montana Department of Environmental Quality implements Montana's environmental laws and provides technical assistance. DEQ is a team of professionals, engineers, and scientists who make timely, accurate, and scientific decisions on complex topics. DEQ works with private industry, the public, and local governments to protect the environment. DEQ 's Energy Planning Section provides state statutes for renewable energy Development, Easement, Bonding, and Decommissioning of Solar, Wind, Hydroelectric and Geothermal projects. For more information on Montana DEQ's Renewable Energy Planning visit deq.mt.gov

The Dawson County Soil Conservation District which administers the Natural Streambed and Land Preservation Act "310 Law" whose goal is to keep rivers and streams in as natural or existing condition as possible, now wants voters to pass Ordinance 2026-01 to expand DCCD's authority and duplicate the duties of the Montana DEQ. Ordinance 2026-01 will create more red tape and regulations for private industry and Dawson County residents who want to invest in renewable energy systems. You may read the proposed Ordinance 2026-01 on the Dawson County Webpage, subject Elections, then Sample June 2 ballot. The ballot ordinance does not contain all the same language as passed by the five DCCD board supervisors, which are all members of the agriculture community, the last sentence reads "agriculture applications are exempt from the ordinance if rated less than fifty (50) kilowatts". If you want a copy of the original ordinance you will have to visit the DCCD office at 102 Fir street in Forest Park as they have no working website. Please vote no on ordinance 2026-01.

Thank You!

Pat Mischel

Glendive

value.Title: 'Unprecedented drought conditions': More than 500 data centers across Nevada , California , and Arizona could feel the pinch as the iconic Hoover Dam potentially loses 40% of its power output because of poor rainfall and abnormally low snowpack numbers
value.Overview: TechRadar Pro, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS & US NEWS, 567words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-W2B3-S690-P3VJ-00000-00'Unprecedented drought conditions': More than 500 data centers across Nevada, California, and Arizona could feel the pinch as the iconic Hoover Dam potentially loses 40% of its power output because of poor rainfall and abnormally low snowpack numbers2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS, RENEWABLE ENERGY NEWS & US NEWS'Unprecedented drought conditions': More than 500 data centers across Nevada, California, and Arizona could feel the pinch as the iconic Hoover Dam potentially loses 40% of its power output because of poor rainfall and abnormally low snowpack numbersEfosa Udinmwen

Hoover Dam power output threatened by severe drought conditions Federal water management plan prioritizes upper basin reservoir stability Colorado River system storage falls to historically low levels

The Hoover Dam, a critical power source for three US states, could see its electricity generation drop by as much as 40% as early as this fall.

Completed in 1936, the dam currently has an installed capacity of 2,078.8 megawatts (approximately 2.08 gigawatts) and produces about 3.3 terawatt hours of energy annually.

The Department of the Interior has announced an emergency drought management plan that will cut water releases from Lake Powell to the minimum legally allowed level.

Upper basin rescue, lower basin sacrifice

This decision, designed to protect Glen Canyon Dam’s ability to produce power, will directly reduce Hoover Dam’s generating capacity by approximately 830 megawatts (0.83 gigawatts), removing roughly 1.32 terawatt hours of annual energy from the regional grid.

Long-term drought has reduced Colorado River system storage to roughly 36% of its total designed capacity, according to the Bureau of Reclamation.

Lake Powell’s inflow forecast sits at just 2.78 million acre-feet, only 29% of the historical average and among the lowest ever recorded.

Without major intervention, Lake Powell could fall below the minimum power pool level of 3,490 feet by August of this year.

The combination of record low snowpack and unprecedented March heat has accelerated the crisis across the entire Colorado River Basin.

To help the situation, Reclamation intends to release between 660,000 and 1 million acre-feet of water from the Flaming Gorge Reservoir between April 2026 and April 2027.

The agency will also reduce the annual release volume from Lake Powell to Lake Mead by 1.48 million acre-feet, bringing it down from 7.48 to 6.0 million acre-feet.

Together, these actions are expected to raise Lake Powell’s elevation by roughly 54 feet, keeping it above the critical 3,490-foot threshold.

However, these actions will lower Lake Mead’s levels further, directly affecting Hoover Dam’s ability to generate power.

Who will feel the impacts?

A loss of 0.83 gigawatts of hydroelectric capacity will force utilities to find replacement power sources, likely turning to natural gas or renewable energy to fill the gap.

This shift could raise electricity costs for residential and industrial customers across Nevada, California, and Arizona.

The region’s data centers — more than 500 facilities that already operate on thin power margins during peak summer demand — will feel the impact most directly, facing higher prices and potential supply constraints in coming years.

The Southern Nevada Water Authority has acknowledged that the drought announcement reveals the severity of regional challenges.

The Bureau of Reclamation is betting that stabilizing the upper basin will prevent a complete system collapse.

However, lower basin states like Arizona, Nevada, and California will bear the immediate cost of that decision.

A 40% cut to a 2.08 gigawatt facility is not a marginal reduction, and replacing 1.32 terawatt hours of annual hydro generation will require investment in alternative power sources.

No amount of emergency planning can manufacture snowpack that does not exist in the mountains.

Until precipitation patterns change dramatically, the region’s hydroelectric future remains uncertain.

Via Fox 5 Vegas

value.Title: Latham Advises on Numerous Energy and Infrastructure Deals Honored by IJGlobal and Proximo

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHD-FVT3-RSJS-13YG-00000-00Latham Advises on Numerous Energy and Infrastructure Deals Honored by IJGlobal and Proximo2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisLatham Advises on Numerous Energy and Infrastructure Deals Honored by IJGlobal and Proximo

Key Highlights:

* Latham & Watkins advised on 16 landmark energy and infrastructure transactions.

* Firm recognized in IJGlobal Awards across digital infrastructure, oil and gas, power, and renewable energy sectors.

* Latham advised on several transactions honored in Proximo's 2025 Deals of the Year report.

Original Press Release:

May 1 -- Latham & Watkins LLP issued the following news release:

- Firm recognized for its role advising on standout energy and infrastructure transactions.

Latham &Watkins continues to stand as the leader in energy and infrastructure (E&I), advising on a number of deals that were recognized in the 2025 IJGlobal Awards and Proximo 's 2025 Deals of the Year report.

Latham advised on 16 landmark transactions that were honored in the IJGlobal Awards across a number of E&I sectors, including digital infrastructure, oil and gas, power, and renewable energy. These transactions include:

For Proximo 's 2025 Deals of the Year, Latham advised on several transactions, including:

Asia Pacific

* ABS Deal of the Year, APAC: Bayfront VII

*Airport Deal of the Year: Ahmedabad, Guwahati, Jaipur, Lucknow, Mangaluru, Trivandrum & Mumbai Airports PPP Bond Refinancing 2025

*Water Deal of the Year: Maynilad Water Services IPO

Europe

*Biofuels Deal of the Year: Vertex Bioenergy

*Bond Deal of the Year: Vantage Towers Project Bond

*Carbon Capture Deal of the Year: Vrtaverket Stockholm BECCS

Latin America

*Power to X Deal of the Year: La Pampina Solar PV Plant & BESS and Dune BESS

North America

*Biomass Deal of the Year: Montana Renewables' Great Falls Biorefinery Expansion FFB Loan

*Carbon Capture Deal of the Year: High Plains CCS

*Downstream Deal of the Year: NextWave's Pasadena Alkylate Production Facility Refinancing

*Hydrogen Deal of the Year: Bloom Energy and Brookfield Warehouse Facility

*LNG Deal of the Year: Rio Grande LNG Train 4

*Market Impact Award: Hyperion Data Centre Megaproject

*Midstream Deal of the Year: Blackcomb and Traverse Pipeline

*Oil & Gas Deal of the Year: CP2 LNG Terminal Phase 1 & CP Express Pipeline

Proximo

For Proximo's 2025 Deals of the Year, Latham advised on several transactions, including:

Latin America

*Emerging Energy Deal of the Year: GIP's Joint Venture with Vale to acquire Aliana Energia

North America

*Solar Deal of the Year: Arevon's Tax Equity Financing for Ratts 1 and Heirloom Renewables Project

*Emerging Energy Deal of the Year: Bloom Energy and Brookfield Joint Venture Financing

*Project Bond Deal of the Year: MN8 US Solar and BESS Portfolio Private Placement

[Category: Energy, Legal Services, Infrastructure and IT Operations Software, Award Wins]

Source: Latham & Watkins LLP

value.Title: Quantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near Broadview

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JMV-63T3-RYHM-93V8-00000-00Quantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near Broadview2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisQuantica commits further to self-funded power for data center near BroadviewALEX MITCHELLMissoula, Mont.FULL TEXT

Quantica Infrastructure reaffirmed Thursday a “pay our own way” approach to powering its planned 5,100-acre data center campus north of Billings.

In a press release, Quantica pledged to cover the costs of electricity infrastructure and any new generation needed to serve its project amid concerns around how NorthWestern Energy might profit off data centers by pass the costs to Montana ratepayers.

“We're paying our own way for power, without increasing Montanans’ electricity bills for our project,” Quantica Infrastructure CEO John Chesser said in a statement. “We’ll pay the cost of electricity infrastructure and any new power generation needed to serve our project. We’ll continue working with utilities and regulators to ensure the project is responsibly reviewed, permitted and aligned with the community’s priorities.”

The data center, contingent on attaining a customer, plans to rely on a mix of their own behind-the-meter wind and solar assets obtained years ago in addition to power from NorthWestern Energy.

Chesser said they are pursuing an all-of-the-above power portfolio, including traditional energy resources, in addition to battery energy storage. He also noted the "evaluation of emerging energy technologies" to power the data center.

Montana Environmental Information Center director Anne Hedges considered the press release to be a "change in their tune" from what Quantica has relayed to the public in its initial messaging.

"They used to say it would all be renewable energy and storage — the lowest cost energy sources today," Hedges said. "Now they are saying traditional energy which means coal and gas. Those resources impose a huge impact on the state of Montana and across the globe, including climate change, air pollution and water pollution."

She added that the emerging technologies reference mentioned in the press release are a "modern day euphemism for nuclear power." Chesser was previously involved in the reported first nuclear-powered data center in the U.S.

In a response about the referenced emerging energy technologies, Quantica stated: "We are evaluating tools that can make the project more reliable and lower‑carbon over time, including advanced long‑duration storage, grid‑optimization technologies, and other lower‑carbon firm resources. Finally, we appreciate the opportunity to clarify, as many Montanans have concerns and expect these discussions to be honest and straightforward, not clouded with speculation and exaggeration, as some opponents of responsible development are prone to."

NorthWestern has agreed to provide up to 1,000 megawatts to the data center, though initial deployment is planned to be 500 megawatts. Where exactly that power will come from hasn’t been shared publicly. At the start of this year, NorthWestern acquired a 375 megawatt share of Colstrip it will sell power from on the market.

The press release echoes a sentiment shared by Quantica’s leaders months ago about a desire for the company to pay its fair share.

However, the CEO’s statement comes amid a hearing held by the Public Service Commission over a planned merger of NorthWestern and Black Hills Energy. NorthWestern CEO Brian Bird has publicly stated that the merger will give the monopoly utility “better opportunities” around data centers.

Opponents have used that focus on data centers to assess that the merger’s approval would lead to higher costs by facilitating and encouraging further data center development down the line, potentially leading to infrastructure costs shouldered by ratepayers.

The statement also arrives during grassroots animus against data centers. A Gallup poll released Wednesday found 71% of Americans oppose local construction of data centers.

Quantica's press release posted online only mentions the word “data center” once in a description of Big Sky Digital Infrastructure, which plans to build the project.

Chesser has previously stated that they expect to begin leveling ground and placing roads this year. In 2027, the company expects to lay the foundations of some buildings. And by 2028, the company expects to begin drawing power for its data center.

Quantica still hasn't obtained a customer, but the company said it continues to see "strong interest in Montana."

value.Title: Turkish Companies Eye U.S. Expansion at SelectUSA Summit

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-04X3-RS19-62MY-00000-00Turkish Companies Eye U.S. Expansion at SelectUSA Summit2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisTurkish Companies Eye U.S. Expansion at SelectUSA Summit

Link to Image

More than 30 Turkish companies descended on Maryland this week to scout investment prospects spanning renewable energy, robotics, health care technology, and dental services at the SelectUSA Investment Summit, held May 3–6 near Washington, D.C.



The annual summit drew thousands of participants — including investors, economic development officials, and sector specialists from over 100 countries.

Tarik Sarvan, chairman of solar technology firm CW Enerji, said his company currently exports between $5 and $6 million in products monthly from Türkiye to the U.S. and is evaluating investment opportunities on a state-by-state basis. "We are trying to create a gateway where we can sell our products," he said, noting that manufacturing would stay in Türkiye while any U.S. presence would center on assembly and distribution.

Bakit Baydaliev, CEO of DOF Robotics, said the U.S. already represents 40 percent of the firm's revenues and is climbing. The company intends to scale its immersive entertainment offerings across several states, including Montana, Wyoming, and Colorado.

RealWorks Product and Marketing Director Fulya Bayram said the company is focused on expanding its AI-driven health care platform HaloScape in the U.S. and deepening relationships with state authorities and technology giants including Nvidia. "Our goal this year is to slightly increase our market share in the US, strengthen Türkiye's representation here, and build close relationships with the states," she said.

Dental startup dentalPrices and software firm IWRobotx also participated, both identifying the U.S. as central to their international expansion ambitions. Dr. Efe Celebi, founder of dental chain Dentgroup, said dentalPrices has already launched operations in Budapest and plans to enter the U.S. market before year-end.

Juliet Abdel, president of the Cedar Rapids Metro Economic Alliance, highlighted her region's competitive advantages for foreign investors — including lower operating costs, affordable living, and multimodal logistics infrastructure, with particular growth potential in aerospace, avionics, and the bioeconomy. "The industries which really overlap with our interests were energy, defense and manufacturing…I'll continue following up on the opportunities that came up in our one-on-one meetings," she said.

Chris Chung, CEO of The Economic Development Partnership of North Carolina, praised Turkish companies as "globally minded," adding: "Türkiye is a big market in and of itself, but there's a lot of opportunities sitting outside of Türkiye, including to both sell and produce products in the United States, and that's where the intersection of opportunity sits for us."

value.Title: Turkish firms explore investment opportunities at US summit

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-KXR3-S7V0-K3H2-00000-00Turkish firms explore investment opportunities at US summit2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:47ZLexisNexisTurkish firms explore investment opportunities at US summit

Turkish businesspeople met with representatives from US states during the SelectUSA Investment Summit this week to explore investment opportunities across sectors ranging from renewable energy and robotics to health care technology and dental services.

The summit, held May 3-6 in Maryland near Washington, DC, brought together thousands of participants, including investors, economic development officials and industry experts from over 100 countries.

More than 30 Turkish companies attended the event.

Tarik Sarvan, chairman of leading Turkish solar technology company CW Enerji, said the firm exports around $5-$6 million worth of products monthly from Türkiye to the US and is considering investment opportunities on a state-by-state basis.

“We are trying to create a gateway where we can sell our products,” he said, stressing that production would remain in Türkiye while potential US operations would focus on assembly and sales.

Bakit Baydaliev, CEO of technology firm DOF Robotics, said the US already accounts for 40% of the company’s market and that the share is growing steadily. The company plans to expand its immersive entertainment businesses across multiple US states, including in Montana, Wyoming and Colorado.

RealWorks Product and Marketing Director Fulya Bayram said the company aims to expand the US footprint of its AI-powered health care platform HaloScape and strengthen ties with state officials and major firms such as Nvidia.

“Our goal this year is to slightly increase our market share in the US, strengthen Türkiye’s representation here, and build close relationships with the states,” she said.

Other Turkish firms attending the summit included dental technology startup dentalPrices and software company IWRobotx, both of which said they view the US market as key to global expansion.

Dr. Efe Celebi, founder of the dental clinic chain Dentgroup, said his company dentalPrices has expanded into Europe with operations in Budapest and is now planning to enter the US market by the end of the year.

Juliet Abdel, president of the Cedar Rapids Metro Economic Alliance, said the company offers foreign investors lower business costs, affordable living and strong transportation access as a midwestern hub, with growth potential in aerospace and avionics, the bioeconomy and technology.

"The industries which really overlap with our interests were energy, defense and manufacturing…I'll continue following up on the opportunities that came up in our one-on-one meetings," she said about her meetings with investors.

Chris Chung, CEO of The Economic Development Partnership of North Carolina, described Turkish companies as “globally minded.”

“Türkiye is a big market in and of itself, but there's a lot of opportunities sitting outside of Türkiye, including to both sell and produce products in the United States, and that's where the intersection of opportunity sits for us,” he said.

value.Title: Bonner data center company seeks 100MW facility

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-9693-RX9H-F11K-00000-00Bonner data center company seeks 100MW facility2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisAPg. 03Bonner data center company seeks 100MW facilityGriffen Smith Lee Newspapers

MISSOULA The developers of a proposed artificial intelligence data center in Bonner told Missoula County the facility could use up 100 megawatts after a “full build out” of the site, according to public records filed this week.

Data center company Krambu announced in March that it seeks to use vacant space within the Bonner Mill Industrial Park for an “AI factory”, which still needs to undergo a special zoning review before the plans can move forward.

While Krambu representatives initially told Bonner residents the development would start at 3 megawatts, the company also acknowledged it could grow the center over several years.

In a supplemental information packet to the county, Krambu-contracted company Dobler Engineering wrote that the space currently has capacity for 29 megawatts.

“It’s important to note that full build out of the site may take two to three years, and that depends on the ultimate amount of power available to the site (possibly up to 100 MW) and the market demand,” the company wrote in the document on April 27.

One-hundred megawatts could power somewhere between 20,000 and 100,000 homes, depending on the power source, and would generally be classified as an average-sized data center, according to IBM.

Lee Newspapers first reported on Krambu’s initial application through a public records request, which showed the company does not plan to regularly staff the site once construction is completed.

Krambu CEO Steve Wood did not return a call from Lee Newspapers on Friday seeking comment.

Missoula County has requested additional information from Krambu before a public review process can begin on the proposal. On Wednesday, county planner Jennie Dixon told the company that more work needs to be done on the possible noise, visual, water, and heat criteria.

“With materials submitted thus far, I do not have enough information to make a favorable recommendation to the Land Use Board on the special exception,” Dixon wrote to Krambu on April 29. “My goal is to assist you in preparing an application that will address all potential concerns that might be raised in review of the special exception.”

The county said on its website that the earliest a special exception review hearing could be held before the Missoula County Consolidated Land Use Board would be on July 1.

The land use board would address whether the proposal would create a negative impact on nearby residents. The proposal does not need approval from the Board of Missoula County Commissioners to proceed.

County regulations also require Krambu secure a new source of renewable energy to supply the project and recycle electronic waste. Those regulations came in response to a cryptocurrency mining operation at the site several years ago.

In its most recent filings with the county, Dobler Engineering said Krambu’s water use would involve a closed loop system that limits the amount of water it uses to about 2,000 gallons per year.

“The evaporative coolers are constructed like a car radiator and drip water over the cooling fluid lines. The water is collected in a reservoir at the bottom and recirculated,” the document said.

The system would require evaporative cooling towers to be built on the exterior of the Bonner mill building. Each tower would cool the equivalent of 4 to 5 megawatts, according to the application.

Currently, Dobler Engineering said 10 towers would be required for the current energy capacity of the facility. The cooling structures would not be taller than the existing building.

The data center would be powered 24 hours a day and have a safeguard system to keep the operation active in the event of maintenance issues, but the company does not plan to have a backup power generator on site, according to the application.

Krambu proposes a row of street trees to minimize the impact of new construction on nearby residences. Wood also told Bonner residents he hopes to add additional uses to the site, like a hydroponics garden.

A petition on change.org urging the county to reject the permits for the data center had secured roughly 2,400 signatures as of Friday afternoon.

Data centers have drawn more public attention in the last two years as applications for them increase across the country, including in talking points for candidates running for public office in Montana.

More information can also be found at MissoulaCountyVoice.com.

Community members write down questions for Krambu employees at St. Ann’s Catholic Church in Bonner, Friday, March 20. The national data center company hopes to bring its 3-megawatt operation to the Bonner Industrial Mill Site.SHANNA MADISON, Missoulian Steve Wood, CEO of Krambu and Paradox Infrastructure, answers questions about a proposed data center facility at Bonner Industrial Mill Park during a Q&A session at St. Ann’s Catholic Church in Bonner, Friday, March 20.SHANNA MADISON, Missoulian

value.Title: Trump administration eases curbs on industries, seeks to boost drilling, mining on taxpayer-owned land

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-44C3-S4RP-X2D6-00000-00Trump administration eases curbs on industries, seeks to boost drilling, mining on taxpayer-owned land2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisTrump administration eases curbs on industries, seeks to boost drilling, mining on taxpayer-owned land

The Interior Department on Tuesday said it is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10 per cent of land in the US. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

Rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres of land

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land - preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands - meaning restoration leases - to a position of prominence.

Trump has pursued flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months cancelled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

value.Title: Experts: Data centers make power grid planning hard
value.Overview: Statesman Journal (Salem, Oregon), NEWS; Pg. A1, 1445words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-MT33-RTGT-N1JG-00000-00Experts: Data centers make power grid planning hard2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:10ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A1Experts: Data centers make power grid planning hardByMonica SamayoaOregon Public Broadcasting

The proliferation of power-hungry data centers is making it difficult to predict the future of the Pacific Northwest's energy needs.

That has implications for the region's efforts to invest in renewable energy sources, and may affect the cost of electricity for consumers in the years ahead.

So far, Oregon has more than 120 data centers across the state, and more are in development.

According to an analysis published in April by Energy + Environmental Economics, the region is expected to build data centers that consume about 3 gigawatts of electricity by 2030 - enough energy to power 2.25 million homes.

But other experts predict data centers could demand far less electricity than that.

A single data center might require as much energy as it takes to power a small city like Ashland, where about 21,000 people live.

Another might need as much energy as it takes to power Eugene, which has nearly 10 times as many residents.

It depends on the size of the data center and whether it's powering artificial intelligence or hosting servers for cloud storage.

With multiple technology companies in different stages of developing data centers across the Northwest, it's impossible to get a precise estimate of just how much energy these high-tech projects will collectively require.

Elaine Hart, co-founder and principal at Sylvan Energy Analytics based in Portland, said that's making it hard to develop plans for an electrical grid that meets the region's needs.

"We're kind of at a point of unprecedented uncertainty, which is very scary for planners, and it means we need solutions that are kind of robust to a lot of different possibilities," she said.

Growing energy gap

In April, Energy + Environmental Economics, known as E3, released the second phase of a study that looks into the greater Pacific Northwest's long-term energy needs. The study was commissioned by 22 electric utilities across the region to look at energy resources in Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Montana, and portions of Utah and Wyoming.

It found that much of the region's need to bring new electricity sources online is unrelated to data centers - population growth and economic development are pushing up energy demand, and the region is not moving fast enough to address energy gaps left by retired coal power plants.

Add a modest amount of data center expansion in the Pacific Northwest to the equation, and the six-state region is facing a 9-gigawatt energy gap by 2030.

That's the average amount of energy consumed by the entire state of Oregon, and if power companies don't find new sources of electricity to meet that growing need, the region's grid could struggle to deliver enough energy to power electric heaters during extreme cold or cooling systems during heat waves.

Looking further ahead, the E3 study says the gap between how much energy the region needs and how much is available could double, to as high as 18 gigawatts by 2035.

"We find that, in the near term, we really aren't on track to fill that gap, and there's a wide variety of market and institutional and other barriers that have really gotten in the way of the industry and the region," E3's senior partner Arne Olsen said during a webinar in April.

And these estimates could be much higher, "if we assumed a more aggressive data center build out in our study," Olsen said.

Unclogging an energy bottleneck

Oregon and Washington face major challenges that could affect how well these states are able to meet the energy needs of the future.

An OPB and ProPublica investigation found that the Bonneville Power Administration, which owns 75% of the region's power grid, has a sluggish process for adding new transmission lines necessary for developers of new electric power sources.

That's slowed efforts to develop large-scale wind and solar energy projects to meet the region's needs. Oregon's and Washington's renewable power development ranks near the bottom in the nation due to the lack of available transmission lines.

E3's report called that transmission bottleneck an obstacle to addressing future energy needs west of the Oregon and Washington Cascades, where energy demand is already strained and expected to grow significantly more.

Building transmission will be key to unclogging that bottleneck, it found.

Both states have laws that require their electric utilities to deliver carbon-free energy by mid-century, but the slow-moving pace of permitting and siting of these developments is contributing to regional power gaps.

The Trump administration has also rescinded billions of dollars originally allocated toward climate action that would have helped both states accelerate the addition of renewable energy sources.

And while both states are moving away from fossil fuel-generated electricity, E3's analysis found the region may need to hold on to natural gas for a little longer.

They found that gas-fired plants could help with grid reliability during peak energy moments.

Natural gas power plants generate more than a third of the electricity consumed in Oregon, and about a fifth of the electricity consumed in Washington.

But the study's authors cautioned against overbuilding new natural gas plants, which could push up already-high electric bills.

Hart, with Sylvan Energy Analytics, agreed that natural gas has an important role to play in the near term. But she said it should be a last resort when considering new sources of electricity.

"If we've got investments in natural gas happening because of data center demand, that's a tough one," she said. "We need to be clear-eyed about and thoughtful about who's paying for it, and what's being done to reduce the utilization of those plants. The best thing we can do to reduce utilization of natural gas plants in the region is to build renewable energy."

Data center demand response

Sylvan Energy Analytics did an independent study looking into E3's initial findings.

That study also forecast a major gap between what the six-state region will need and how much energy is already being generated. It found the gap could be smaller than the 9 gigawatt energy gap E3 predicts, however.

The independent study predicted a 6 gigawatt gap - but said the gap could be as low as 1 gigawatt.

That huge range in forecasts, driven by uncertainty around how many data centers may be built and how much energy they will use, makes it hard for grid planners to determine how many more renewable energy developments the region may need to build out.

And if the region overbuilds these developments, those costs could show up on utility bills - hurting ratepayers, Hart said.

As power companies and policymakers look to generate more electricity and build powerlines to meet the energy demand of the future, the Sylvan Energy Analytics report maps out another way to shrink the gap between how much power is available and how much the region needs.

It sees energy efficiency, driven by developing technology that uses less electricity to perform the same task, as a key tool.

Another high-tech solution is known as "demand response," which means using less power during peak moments. Some technology already uses demand response, like smart thermostats that adjust the amount of energy used.

Hart said the same concept should be taken up by data centers during the cold winter or hot summer days, when energy use is highest.

Tech companies with data centers around the globe could shift computing tasks to other locations when the grid is strained. That could significantly help the region save energy.

"If you have the ability to call the data centers first, in that sort of emergency circumstance, and curtail their output before affecting residential and small commercial customers, under some of the scenarios we might be OK," she said. "We might only need to build a little bit more than what's already sort of coming down the pike."

This type of power flexibility is already widely being used in Texas, she said. But that required state legislation. Google says it has tried a demand response pilot in The Dalles.

Hart said she doesn't expect enough data centers to power down voluntarily to address Oregon's needs.

"I don't think they'll do it on their own," she said. "It might require legislation, and other states are taking up this question as well."

But during Oregon's short 2026 legislative session, bills focused on helping the grid, with solutions like balcony solar and virtual power plants, did not make it to Gov. Tina Kotek's desk for signature.

The virtual power plant bill would have helped relieve some strain on the grid by harnessing and distributing energy generated in homes during high peak moments.

It's unclear if Oregon lawmakers plan on bringing up the bills again next year.

value.Title: Trump ends conservation use rule for public lands

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJV-PFH3-RX76-S1V5-00000-00Trump ends conservation use rule for public lands2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisTrump ends conservation use rule for public landsIndia

India, May 13 -- The Interior Department is cancelling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10 per cent of land in the US. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land - preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands - meaning restoration leases - to a position of prominence.

The federal Government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states, including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidised under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published on Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months cancelled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves - such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy - across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometres). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and, for more than a century, has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

May 11, 2026 - Dividend Announcement 1 of 2:

AVISTA QUARTERLY DIVIDENDS STEADY

Avista today announced a quarterly dividend of 49.25c per share for Q1/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q4/2025) dividend of 49.25c. The ex-dividend date is Tuesday, May 19, 2026 and the record date is Tuesday, May 19, 2026 and it is payable on Friday, June 12. Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended May 11 were $1.96. This reflects actual dividend yield of 4.8% at the last price of $40.82.

ACTIVITIES:

Avista Corporation, a utility holding company headquartered in Spokane, Washington, and listed on the New York Stock Exchange (ticker: AVA), focuses on providing reliable energy services in the Pacific Northwest. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through its subsidiary Avista Utilities, which serves approximately 400,000 electric and 360,000 natural gas customers across Washington, Idaho, and parts of Oregon and Montana. The company operates a diverse energy portfolio that includes hydroelectric, thermal, and wind-powered facilities, emphasizing efficient resource management and infrastructure maintenance. In recent years, Avista's fastest-growing segments have been its renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, fueled by rising demand for sustainable options and supportive regulatory policies. This strategic expansion enhances Avista's competitive position in the evolving energy sector while supporting environmental goals and customer needs.

May 11, 2026 - Dividend Announcement 2 of 2:

ORMAT TECHNOLOGIES QUARTERLY DIVIDENDS STEADY

Ormat Technologies today announced a quarterly dividend of 12.0c per share for Q1/2026; unchanged from the previous quarter (Q4/2025) dividend of 12.0c. The ex-dividend date is Wednesday, May 20, 2026 and the record date is Wednesday, May 20, 2026 and it is payable on Wednesday, June 03. Total dividends per share paid in the 12 months ended May 11 were 48.0c. This reflects actual dividend yield of 0.4% at the last price of $121.67.

ACTIVITIES:

Ormat Technologies, Inc., a U.S.-listed company on the New York Stock Exchange, is a global leader in renewable energy solutions, specializing in the development, construction, ownership, and operation of geothermal power plants. The company's core activities encompass geothermal energy generation, recovered energy power systems that convert waste heat into electricity, and energy storage solutions, including battery systems that support grid stability and renewable integration. Ormat's fastest-growing segments include its energy storage business, driven by increasing demand for reliable, scalable solutions amid the global shift to sustainable energy, as well as expansions in recovered energy projects that leverage industrial waste heat for efficient power production. This strategic focus has positioned Ormat as a key player in the transition to cleaner energy sources, with a strong track record of operational excellence and innovative technology deployment across international markets.
