ACTIVITIES:

Eversource Energy, a leading utility company listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), operates primarily in the northeastern United States, delivering reliable electric power and natural gas services to millions of customers. The company's core activities encompass electric transmission and distribution, natural gas delivery, and water services, with a strong emphasis on infrastructure maintenance and energy efficiency initiatives. In recent years, Eversource has experienced rapid growth in its renewable energy segments, particularly offshore wind projects and clean energy solutions, driven by increasing demand for sustainable power sources and supportive regulatory environments. These expansions, including investments in large-scale wind farms and electric vehicle charging infrastructure, have positioned the company as a key player in the transition to greener energy, while enhancing its competitive edge and long-term revenue potential. Overall, Eversource's strategic focus on innovation and sustainability supports its commitment to operational excellence and community service.

AMERICAN RESULTS

Avista (NYSE:AVA), announced total revenue of $570m for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 6.9% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and down 7.6% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025].

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 16.5% from $79.0m to $92.0m

- EPS growth 13.3% from $0.98 to $1.11

- EBIT to Sales was up from 21.1% to 25.4%

- Profit before tax to Sales was up from 14.7% to 18.4%

- Profit after tax to Sales was up from 12.8% to 16.1%

Unfavourable Changes:

- Total revenue was down 7.6% from $617.0m to $570.0m

- Interest expenses to Sales was up from 6.3% to 7%

Note:

- Tax expenses to Sales was up from 1.9% to 2.3%

ACTIVITIES:

Avista Corporation, a utility holding company headquartered in Spokane, Washington, and listed on the New York Stock Exchange (ticker: AVA), focuses on providing reliable energy services in the Pacific Northwest. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through its subsidiary Avista Utilities, which serves approximately 400,000 electric and 360,000 natural gas customers across Washington, Idaho, and parts of Oregon and Montana. The company operates a diverse energy portfolio that includes hydroelectric, thermal, and wind-powered facilities, emphasizing efficient resource management and infrastructure maintenance. In recent years, Avista's fastest-growing segments have been its renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, fueled by rising demand for sustainable options and supportive regulatory policies. This strategic expansion enhances Avista's competitive position in the evolving energy sector while supporting environmental goals and customer needs.

MARKET POSITION:

Avista Corporation (NYSE:AVA), the NYSE's 21st largest Utility company by market cap, increased $1.01 (or 2.5%) in the trailing 12 months to May 05, 2026 to close at $40.93. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 27.6% over the same period, this is a relative price change of -25.1%. Average daily volume of 661,092 shares in the trailing year was 1.2 times average daily volume in the previous year.

urn:contentItem:6JGH-8453-S3MR-41C4-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: NorthWestern Energy Group reports Net Profit of $63.5m Corporate Wire Date: 30 April 2026 10:41 EDT Release Date: April 29, 20262026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: NorthWestern Energy Group reports Net Profit of $63.5m Corporate Wire Date: 30 April 2026 10:41 EDT Release Date: April 29, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

NorthWestern Energy Group (NASDAQ:NWE) announced net profit for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026] of $63.5m.

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Total revenue was up 6.6% from $466.6m to $497.6m

Unfavourable Changes:

- Net profit was down 17.4% from $76.9m to $63.5m

- EPS was down 17.6% from $1.25 to $1.03

- EBITDA to Revenues was down from 33.1% to 29%

- EBIT to Revenues was down from 19.7% to 15.5%

- Profit before tax to Revenues was down from 19.7% to 15.5%

- Profit after tax to Revenues was down from 16.5% to 12.8%

ACTIVITIES:

NorthWestern Energy Group Inc., a Nasdaq-listed utility company headquartered in Sioux Falls, South Dakota, is a leading provider of regulated electric and natural gas services primarily in Montana, South Dakota, and Nebraska. The company's core business revolves around the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity and natural gas, serving residential, commercial, and industrial customers while emphasizing reliable infrastructure and environmental stewardship. A key focus includes integrating renewable energy sources, such as wind and hydroelectric power, which represent the fastest-growing segments of its operations. This expansion is driven by increasing demand for sustainable energy solutions and regulatory incentives, positioning the company for enhanced market share and revenue growth. NorthWestern Energy maintains a strong commitment to operational efficiency, safety, and community engagement, supporting long-term stability in the competitive energy sector.

MARKET POSITION:

NorthWestern Energy Group Inc. (NASDAQ:NWE), the NASDAQ's 8th largest Utility company by market cap, jumped $14.39 (or 25.3%) in the trailing 12 months to April 29, 2026 to close at $71.34. Compared with the NASDAQ-100 Index which has risen 39.9% over the same period, this is a relative price change of -14.7%. Average daily volume of 479,508 shares in the trailing year was 1.3 times average daily volume in the previous year.

value.Title: Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands
value.Overview: Associated Press State & Local, BUSINESS NEWS; DOMESTIC NEWS; POLITICAL NEWS; STATE AND REGIONAL, 436words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJH-6C93-RXCN-Y4NG-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

value.Title: Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-9KW3-RT1Y-C255-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS,INTERNATIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMatthew BrownThe Associated Press

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJM-5YX3-RT70-T28K-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisIRAN NEWSTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMatthew Brown

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land - preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands - meaning restoration leases - to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves - such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy - across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJH-67W3-RXCN-Y3PX-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJH-6BH3-RXCN-Y4GB-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JHV-2T23-S4NP-P04S-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Avista reports 30% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 05 May 2026 23:33 EDT Release Date: May 05, 20262026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:10ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Avista reports 30% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 05 May 2026 23:33 EDT Release Date: May 05, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

Avista (NYSE:AVA), announced net profit of $92.0m for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 30% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 16% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025]. Earnings per share (EPS) were up 27.6% sequentially from 87.0c in Q4/2025 to $1.11 in Q1/2026.

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 16.5% from $79.0m to $92.0m

- EPS growth 13.3% from $0.98 to $1.11

- EBIT to Sales was up from 21.1% to 25.4%

- Profit before tax to Sales was up from 14.7% to 18.4%

- Profit after tax to Sales was up from 12.8% to 16.1%

Unfavourable Changes:

- Total revenue was down 7.6% from $617.0m to $570.0m

- Interest expenses to Sales was up from 6.3% to 7%

Note:

- Tax expenses to Sales was up from 1.9% to 2.3%

ACTIVITIES:

Avista Corporation, a utility holding company headquartered in Spokane, Washington, and listed on the New York Stock Exchange (ticker: AVA), focuses on providing reliable energy services in the Pacific Northwest. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through its subsidiary Avista Utilities, which serves approximately 400,000 electric and 360,000 natural gas customers across Washington, Idaho, and parts of Oregon and Montana. The company operates a diverse energy portfolio that includes hydroelectric, thermal, and wind-powered facilities, emphasizing efficient resource management and infrastructure maintenance. In recent years, Avista's fastest-growing segments have been its renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, fueled by rising demand for sustainable options and supportive regulatory policies. This strategic expansion enhances Avista's competitive position in the evolving energy sector while supporting environmental goals and customer needs.

MARKET POSITION:

Avista Corporation (NYSE:AVA), the NYSE's 21st largest Utility company by market cap, increased $1.01 (or 2.5%) in the trailing 12 months to May 05, 2026 to close at $40.93. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 27.6% over the same period, this is a relative price change of -25.1%. Average daily volume of 661,092 shares in the trailing year was 1.2 times average daily volume in the previous year.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CSY3-SCTW-62K4-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JHC-YV13-SHFW-V4WN-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Avista reports 30% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 05 May 2026 23:33 EDT Release Date: May 05, 20262026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:10ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Avista reports 30% sequential rise in Quarterly Net Profit Corporate Wire Date: 05 May 2026 23:33 EDT Release Date: May 05, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

Avista (NYSE:AVA), announced net profit of $92.0m for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 30% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 16% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025]. Earnings per share (EPS) were up 27.6% sequentially from 87.0c in Q4/2025 to $1.11 in Q1/2026.

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 16.5% from $79.0m to $92.0m

- EPS growth 13.3% from $0.98 to $1.11

- EBIT to Sales was up from 21.1% to 25.4%

- Profit before tax to Sales was up from 14.7% to 18.4%

- Profit after tax to Sales was up from 12.8% to 16.1%

Unfavourable Changes:

- Total revenue was down 7.6% from $617.0m to $570.0m

- Interest expenses to Sales was up from 6.3% to 7%

Note:

- Tax expenses to Sales was up from 1.9% to 2.3%

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] EPS was up 13.3%, Revenue was down 7.6% and Net Profit was up 16.5%.

ACTIVITIES:

Avista Corporation, a utility holding company headquartered in Spokane, Washington, and listed on the New York Stock Exchange (ticker: AVA), focuses on providing reliable energy services in the Pacific Northwest. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through its subsidiary Avista Utilities, which serves approximately 400,000 electric and 360,000 natural gas customers across Washington, Idaho, and parts of Oregon and Montana. The company operates a diverse energy portfolio that includes hydroelectric, thermal, and wind-powered facilities, emphasizing efficient resource management and infrastructure maintenance. In recent years, Avista's fastest-growing segments have been its renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, fueled by rising demand for sustainable options and supportive regulatory policies. This strategic expansion enhances Avista's competitive position in the evolving energy sector while supporting environmental goals and customer needs.

MARKET POSITION:

Avista Corporation (NYSE:AVA), the NYSE's 21st largest Utility company by market cap, increased $1.01 (or 2.5%) in the trailing 12 months to May 05, 2026 to close at $40.93. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 27.6% over the same period, this is a relative price change of -25.1%. Average daily volume of 661,092 shares in the trailing year was 1.2 times average daily volume in the previous year.

value.Title: Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands
value.Overview: The Associated Press, BUSINESS NEWS; DOMESTIC NEWS; POLITICAL NEWS; STATE AND REGIONAL, 557words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJK-CTM3-SCTW-63FD-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-CW13-SD69-R46P-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

value.Title: Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJK-CTR3-SCTW-63G9-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisBUSINESS NEWSDOMESTIC NEWSPOLITICAL NEWSSTATE AND REGIONALTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWNAssociated PressBILLINGS, Mont.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.

The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.

But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.

Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.

Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded."

In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases.

Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.

“This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement.

The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.

The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday.

It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.

In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

SECTION 2 CORPORATE PROFILE

2.1 Activities

Avista Corporation, a utility holding company headquartered in Spokane, Washington, and listed on the New York Stock Exchange (ticker: AVA), focuses on providing reliable energy services in the Pacific Northwest. Its core business activities encompass the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity and natural gas, primarily through its subsidiary Avista Utilities, which serves approximately 400,000 electric and 360,000 natural gas customers across Washington, Idaho, and parts of Oregon and Montana. The company operates a diverse energy portfolio that includes hydroelectric, thermal, and wind-powered facilities, emphasizing efficient resource management and infrastructure maintenance. In recent years, Avista's fastest-growing segments have been its renewable energy initiatives, such as wind and solar projects, fueled by rising demand for sustainable options and supportive regulatory policies. This strategic expansion enhances Avista's competitive position in the evolving energy sector while supporting environmental goals and customer needs. It is the NYSE's 24th largest Utility company by market capitalisation.

SECTION 3 PAST YEAR PRESS RELEASES

3.1 Press Releases and Corporate Wire

Press Release article 1 of 5, Source: Avista Corporation, 300 words

May 13: Regions first microgrid begins operations at Spokane community center

SPOKANE, Wash., May 12, 2026 -- Avista Utilities today announced the start of operations for the regionsfirst community-based microgrid. This marks the launch of an innovative energy system designed to help the Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Family Outreach Center (MLK Center) stay open and continue serving the community during extended, unplanned power outages.

The Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Family Outreach Center is a non-profit, community- based social service center located in East Central Spokane, one of the most ethnically diverse neighborhoods in Spokane County.

Located at the community center, the microgrid brings together solar power, battery storage and natural gas to provide added reliability when the power goes out, such as during extreme weather or other emergencies.

The systems solar and battery storage will also help the MLK Center reduce its standard energy bill, allowing for more resources to be redirected into critical community programs.

Were here for families every day, and during emergencies, people rely on us even more. This energy system helps us continue that work and keep our doors open for the East Central neighborhood, which means so much to me, said Freda Gandy, MLK Center executive Director.

On a typical day, the MLK Center will continue using power from the grid, supplementing energy from onsite solar and battery storage. During extended outages, batteries can help keep power flowing, with natural gas backup available if an outage lasts longer than a few hours. This helps ensure the Center remains open and operational.

The project was made possible through grant funding from Avistas Named Communities Investment Fund (NCIF) and grant support from the Washington State Department of Commerce.

Avistas NCIF program is the only one of its kind at a Washington utility.

Located at the community center, the microgrid brings together solar power, battery storage and natural gas to provide added reliability when the power goes out, such as during extreme weather or other emergencies.

The systems solar and battery storage will also help the MLK Center reduce its standard energy bill, allowing for more resources to be redirected into critical community programs.

Were here for families every day, and during emergencies, people rely on us even more. This energy system helps us continue that work and keep our doors open for the East Central neighborhood, which means so much to me, said Freda Gandy, MLK Center executive Director.

On a typical day, the MLK Center will continue using power from the grid, supplementing energy from onsite solar and battery storage. During extended outages, batteries can help keep power flowing, with natural gas backup available if an outage lasts longer than a few hours. This helps ensure the Center remains open and operational.

The project was made possible through grant funding from Avistas Named Communities Investment Fund (NCIF) and grant support from the Washington State Department of Commerce.

Avistas NCIF program is the only one of its kind at a Washington utility.

May 06: Avista Corp. Board Declares Common Stock Dividend

SPOKANE, Wash., May 06, 2026 -- Avista Corp.s (NYSE: AVA) board of directors has declared a quarterly dividend of $0.4925 per share on the companys common stock, yielding an annualized dividend of $1.97. The common stock dividend is payable June 12, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on May 19, 2026.

The declaration of dividends is at the sole discretion of the board of directors. The board considers the level of dividends on a regular basis, taking into account numerous factors, including financial results, business strategies, and economic and competitive conditions.

May 05: Avista Corp. Reports Q1 2026 Financial Results, Confirms 2026 Utility Earnings Guidance

SPOKANE, Wash., May 05, 2026 -- Avista Corp. (NYSE: AVA) today reported net income based on GAAP of $92 million, or $1.11 per diluted share, for the first quarter of 2026, compared to $79 million, or $0.98 per diluted share, in 2025. Non-GAAP utility earnings1 were $91 million, or $1.10 per diluted share, compared to $82 million, or $1.01 per diluted share in 2025. Avista Corp. is confirming its 2026 non-GAAP utility earnings guidance2 with a range of $2.52 to $2.72 per diluted share.

CEO Perspective

Strong performance in the first quarter demonstrates our focus on fundamentals: safety, reliability, and sound operational and financial execution. Our continued investments ensure we meet the needs of the communities we serve and also build long-term value for our customers, communities and shareholders. We are on track to meet our 2026 earnings guidance and are confident in the opportunities ahead, said Heather Rosentrater, President and CEO of Avista.

Analysis of First Quarter 2026 GAAP Earnings

Net income for the first quarter of 2026 increased compared to the first quarter of 2025 primarily due to increased utility margin resulting from the effects of our general rate cases and net investment gains at our other businesses compared to net investment losses in the first quarter of 2025.

Analysis of 2026 Non-GAAP Utility Earnings

The following table presents the changes in non-GAAP utility earnings and non-GAAP utility earnings per diluted share for the first quarter of 2026, as compared to the first quarter of 2025. It also outlines the various after-tax factors that contributed to these changes (dollars in millions, except per-share data):

(a)The tax impact of each line item was calculated using Avista Corp.'s federal statutory tax rate of 21 percent.

(b)Electric utility margin decreased as a result of the removal of revenues related to the recovery of Colstrip costs, partially offset by other effects of our general rates cases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-HGD3-RVPP-N13J-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisNEWSTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWN

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees...

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land...

The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land.The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling.Related video above: The Trump Administration is working to displace 900 bison in Montana, after authorizing grazing permits during his first term.But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands.Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration.Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence.The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states, including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration has also sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden.The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance.It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota.In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles. The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and, for more than a century, has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases. BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. Related video above: The Trump Administration is working to displace 900 bison in Montana, after authorizing grazing permits during his first term. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states, including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration has also sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles. The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and, for more than a century, has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-BRM3-SCMM-W0T5-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisNEWSTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a 'use' of public landsMATTHEW BROWN

BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The...

BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on t...

BILLINGS, Mont. — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the "multiple use" mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the "non-use" of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

urn:contentItem:6JJK-BRM3-SCMM-W112-00000-00Trump administration cancels rule that made conservation a ‘use’ of public lands2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:58:24ZLexisNexisNEWSTrump administration cancels rule that made conservation a ‘use’ of public lands

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump’s administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land....

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump’s administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing...

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump’s administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department’s Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government’s vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden’s administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

BILLINGS, Mont. – The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. BILLINGS, Mont. – The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on t...

BILLINGS, Mont. – The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. Recommended Videos But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Bobby McEnaney with the Natural Resources Defense Council said repealing the rule ”means less protection for the clean drinking water, less protection for endangered wildlife that depend on healthy habitat, and less accountability when corporations leave these landscapes damaged and degraded." In documents released Monday, administration officials said it exceeded the land bureau’s authority for outside parties to be allowed to obtain conservation leases. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. “This action provides greater clarity and predictability for independent oil and natural gas producers—many of whom rely on consistent permitting and leasing processes to operate efficiently and invest in domestic energy supply,” Dan Naatz with the Independent Petroleum Association of America said in a statement. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The repeal is effective 30 days after it is published in the Federal Register, which was scheduled for Tuesday. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing...

BILLINGS, Mont. (AP) — The Interior Department is canceling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10% of land in the U.S. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months canceled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases.

Billings, May 12 (AP) The Interior Department is cancelling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The...

Billings, May 12 (AP) The Interior Department is cancelling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing...

Billings, May 12 (AP) The Interior Department is cancelling a rule that put conservation on equal footing with development, as President Donald Trump's administration eases restrictions on industries and seeks to boost drilling, logging, mining and grazing on taxpayer-owned land. The 2024 rule adopted under former President Joe Biden was meant to refocus the Interior Department's Bureau of Land Management, which oversees about 10 per cent of land in the US. It allowed public property to be leased for restoration in the same way that oil companies lease land for drilling. But Interior Secretary Doug Burgum has said the rule could have blocked access to hundreds of thousands of acres (hectares) of land — preventing energy and timber production and hurting ranchers who graze on public lands. Supporters argued that conservation had long been a secondary consideration at the land bureau, neglecting its mission under the 1976 Federal Lands Policy Management Act. While the bureau previously issued leases for conservation purposes in limited cases, it never had a dedicated program prior to the Biden administration. Industry groups and their Republican allies in Congress strongly opposed the rule and had lobbied to repeal it. They said the change under Biden violated the “multiple use” mandate for Interior Department lands, by catapulting the “non-use” of federal lands — meaning restoration leases — to a position of prominence. The federal government's vast land holdings are concentrated in Western states including Alaska, California, Nevada, New Mexico, Utah and Wyoming. Since taking office, Trump has pursued a flurry of actions aimed at boosting fossil fuel production from those taxpayer-owned sites. The Republican administration also has sought to sideline some renewable energy projects, claiming they were unfairly subsidized under Biden. The official repeal of the rule was scheduled to be published Tuesday in the Federal Register. Documents detailing the action were released in advance. It comes after Republicans in Congress in recent months cancelled land management plans adopted in the closing days of Biden's administration that restricted development in large areas of Alaska, Montana and North Dakota. In addition to its surface land holdings, the Bureau of Land Management regulates publicly owned underground mineral reserves — such as coal for power plants and lithium for renewable energy — across more than 1 million square miles (2.5 million square kilometers). The bureau has a history of industry-friendly policies and for more than a century has sold grazing permits and oil and gas leases. (AP) GSP
